content.text: Test Strategy Aligned to Release Trains

A release train is a fixed-cadence deployment process: code is merged into main continuously, but production deployments happen on a schedule (daily, weekly, or per sprint). Each stop on the train — commit, PR open, merge, deploy to staging, deploy to production — has a corresponding test gate. The test gate determines which tests run at that stop, what must pass to advance, and who is notified if it fails.

The purpose of aligning tests to release trains is not to run all tests everywhere. It is to run the cheapest tests that can catch the most likely regressions at the earliest possible point in the train. An expensive eval run that catches a prompt regression before it merges costs much less than the same regression caught in production.

This sub-subtopic maps the five release-train stops to their test gates, shows the CI configuration for each gate, defines what blocking means at each level, and covers how to keep the train moving when a non-critical gate fails.
The Five Release-Train Gates

attrs.alt: Release train gates: commit → PR → merge → staging → production with test types and timing at each gate

content.text: Stop
Gate
Tests
Blocking?
Typical Duration
Commit (local)
Gate 1
Unit tests (layers 1+2), lint, type check, flag config validation
Yes — developer runs locally before pushing
< 2 minutes
PR opened
Gate 2
Eval suite (when prompts or src change), security scan, PR-level unit tests
Yes — required CI check on the PR; merge blocked if red
2–10 minutes
Merge to main
Gate 3
Integration tests, full eval suite, API cost audit
Yes — deploy pipeline does not start if gate 3 fails
10–30 minutes
Deploy to staging
Gate 4
E2E tests, smoke tests, canary traffic (5–10% of staging)
Yes — canary does not progress to 100% if gate 4 fails
30–60 minutes
Deploy to production
Gate 5
SLO monitoring, canary metrics, automated rollback trigger
Yes — kill switch fires if SLOs breach; canary paused
24 hours to 1 week
Gate 1: Commit-Level (Pre-Push Hook)

Gate 1 runs locally before a developer pushes to the remote. It must be fast — under 2 minutes — or developers will start skipping it. The goal is to catch syntax errors, type errors, and obviously broken unit tests before they reach CI:

# .husky/pre-push
# Runs automatically before every git push.
# Fail fast: exit non-zero to block the push.

set -e

echo "[pre-push] Running gate 1..."

# Unit tests (layers 1 + 2 only — no API calls)
python -m pytest tests/unit/ -q --tb=short

# Type check
mypy src/ --ignore-missing-imports -q

# Lint
ruff check src/ tests/

# Flag config validation (local flags/flags.yaml)
python scripts/validate_flag_configs.py

echo "[pre-push] Gate 1 passed."
Gate 2: PR-Level CI

Gate 2 runs when a PR is opened or updated. It includes the eval suite, but only when files that affect model behaviour have changed. Running evals on every PR — including documentation, config, or test changes — wastes API credits without adding signal:

# .github/workflows/gate-2-pr.yml
name: Gate 2 — PR checks

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  unit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest tests/unit/ -q
      - run: mypy src/ --ignore-missing-imports -q
      - run: ruff check src/ tests/

  evals:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Only run evals if this PR touches model-facing files
    if: |
      contains(join(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, ','), 'prompt-change') ||
      (github.event.pull_request.changed_files > 0)
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run eval suite
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python -m evals.runner \
            --suite evals/cases/ticket_classifier.json \
            --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier_baseline.json

The `prompt-change` label is applied automatically by a PR hook when `prompts/`, `src/`, or `tools/` are in the diff. Teams that use this label workflow can also manually apply it to trigger evals on PRs that change logic indirectly.
Gate 3: Post-Merge CI

Gate 3 runs after a PR merges to main. It is the last gate before the deploy pipeline starts. Gate 3 runs the full integration test suite (not just unit tests) and a cost audit that checks whether the recent commits have increased average token usage beyond a threshold:

# .github/workflows/gate-3-merge.yml
name: Gate 3 — Post-merge validation

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run integration tests
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: pytest tests/integration/ -m integration -n 4 -v

  cost-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run API cost audit
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: python scripts/cost_audit.py --max-monthly-usd 500

# scripts/cost_audit.py
"""
Fetch this month's Anthropic API token usage and compute estimated cost.
Fails if the projected monthly cost exceeds the configured threshold.
This runs post-merge; a failure blocks the deploy pipeline.
"""
import argparse, sys
import anthropic

# Current pricing (USD per million tokens) — update when pricing changes.
_INPUT_COST_PER_M  = 0.25   # claude-haiku-4-5 input
_OUTPUT_COST_PER_M = 1.25   # claude-haiku-4-5 output

def estimate_monthly_cost(input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
    return (input_tokens / 1_000_000 * _INPUT_COST_PER_M +
            output_tokens / 1_000_000 * _OUTPUT_COST_PER_M)

def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--max-monthly-usd", type=float, required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    # Anthropic usage API — returns aggregated token counts
    usage = client.usage.get_tokens()   # hypothetical; use your actual billing API
    cost  = estimate_monthly_cost(usage.input_tokens, usage.output_tokens)
    print(f"Estimated monthly cost: ${cost:.2f} (threshold: ${args.max_monthly_usd:.2f})")
    if cost > args.max_monthly_usd:
        print(f"COST THRESHOLD EXCEEDED — investigate before deploying")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Cost audit passed.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Gate 4 and 5: Staging and Production
Gates 4 and 5 are deployment gates, not CI gates. They run after the deploy and check whether the deployed system behaves correctly under real or simulated traffic:
Gate
Trigger
Test
Pass Criterion
Failure Action
4 — Staging
Deploy to staging completes
E2E Playwright suite + smoke test HTTP calls
All E2E tests pass; HTTP smoke calls return 200
Halt canary; notify on-call; do not deploy to production
5 — Production canary
10% traffic routed to new version
SLO monitoring: p99 latency, error rate, token cost rate
SLOs hold for 24 hours at canary traffic
Automated kill switch or revert deploy; 100% traffic returns to old version

Gate 5 is not a test you write — it is a monitoring configuration you maintain. The 'test' is continuous. Failing gate 5 means the kill switch fires, not that a test suite reports a failure. Design your monitoring dashboards and alerting thresholds before you deploy the first canary, not after.
Handling Non-Blocking Failures
Not every gate failure should stop the train. Some failures are worth investigating but not worth blocking a release:
Latency regressions below the SLO threshold
: if p99 latency increases by 10% but the SLO threshold is 20%, log a warning and continue. Create a ticket for investigation, but do not block the release.
Eval pass rate drop within the tolerance band
: if the pass rate drops from 97% to 96% and the gate is 95%, log the drop and track it over time. A single-point drop may be model variance rather than a regression.
Cost audit in a warning zone
: if estimated monthly cost is 90% of the threshold, send an alert but let the deploy proceed. Block only when the threshold is breached.
Flaky E2E tests

: if a specific E2E test has a known intermittent failure, tag it with `@pytest.mark.flaky` and exclude it from the blocking gate. Flaky tests that block releases incentivise developers to delete them rather than fix them.
Eval Budget Management Across the Train

Eval costs accumulate with every PR that touches model-facing files. A team running 20 PRs a week against a 50-case eval suite at $0.001 per case can spend $1,000 per month on evals alone without noticing. Budgeting eval runs across the train keeps costs predictable:

# scripts/eval_budget_tracker.py
"""
Track eval API spend across the current billing month.
Each eval run logs its token usage to a JSONL file.
The budget tracker reads the file and warns when usage approaches the threshold.
Run this at the start of each eval run (gate 2 and gate 3) to pre-check budget.
"""
import json, sys
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

_LOG_PATH   = Path("eval_usage_log.jsonl")
_BUDGET_USD = 200.0   # monthly eval budget

# Pricing (USD per million tokens) for claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
_INPUT_COST_PER_M  = 0.25
_OUTPUT_COST_PER_M = 1.25

def log_eval_run(suite_name: str, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> None:
    """Append a run record to the usage log. Call after each eval run."""
    record = {
        "timestamp":     datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "suite":         suite_name,
        "input_tokens":  input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": output_tokens,
        "cost_usd":      (input_tokens / 1_000_000 * _INPUT_COST_PER_M +
                          output_tokens / 1_000_000 * _OUTPUT_COST_PER_M),
    }
    with _LOG_PATH.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

def current_month_spend() -> float:
    """Sum eval costs for the current calendar month."""
    if not _LOG_PATH.exists():
        return 0.0
    now   = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    total = 0.0
    for line in _LOG_PATH.read_text().splitlines():
        try:
            rec = json.loads(line)
            ts  = datetime.fromisoformat(rec["timestamp"])
            if ts.year == now.year and ts.month == now.month:
                total += rec.get("cost_usd", 0.0)
        except (json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError, ValueError):
            pass
    return total

def check_budget_before_run(estimated_cost: float) -> bool:
    """
    Return True if there is enough budget remaining for this run.
    Estimated cost is (cases * avg_tokens * price_per_token).
    """
    spent = current_month_spend()
    remaining = _BUDGET_USD - spent
    if estimated_cost > remaining:
        print(
            f"EVAL BUDGET EXCEEDED: spent ${spent:.2f}, "
            f"remaining ${remaining:.2f}, "
            f"this run estimated ${estimated_cost:.2f}. "
            f"Skipping eval run."
        )
        return False
    print(f"Eval budget OK: ${spent:.2f} spent, ${remaining:.2f} remaining.")
    return True

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--report", action="store_true")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    if args.report:
        print(f"Current month eval spend: ${current_month_spend():.2f} / ${_BUDGET_USD:.2f}")

The log is a JSONL file committed to the repository. Each CI job that runs evals appends to it at the end of the run. The budget check at the start of a run skips the eval job rather than letting it run and then billing the organisation. Monthly budget resets are handled by the date filter in `current_month_spend()` — no cron job needed.
Post-Incident Gate Additions

Every production incident that was not caught by an existing gate should result in a gate addition. The addition might be a new eval case (if the incident was a model behaviour regression), a new integration test assertion (if it was a structural bug), or a new SLO threshold (if it was a performance incident):

# scripts/incident_gate_checklist.py
"""
Post-incident gate addition checklist generator.
For each incident field, this script prints what needs to be added to
which gate. Run after completing a post-mortem to produce an action list.

Usage:
    python scripts/incident_gate_checklist.py \
        --type model_behaviour \
        --description "Refusal threshold shifted for legal advice prompts" \
        --affected_gate 5 \
        --root_cause "Prompt whitespace change shifted model attention"
"""
import argparse, textwrap

GATE_ADDITIONS = {
    "model_behaviour": {
        "add_to": "eval suite (gate 2 + gate 3)",
        "action": (
            "Add a new eval case to evals/cases/ that reproduces the regression. "
            "Use a real prompt from the incident (anonymised). "
            "Update the baseline. "
            "The eval must PASS at the fixed commit and FAIL at the regressed commit."
        ),
    },
    "structural_bug": {
        "add_to": "integration tests (gate 3)",
        "action": (
            "Add an integration test that asserts the structural property that was violated. "
            "Example: if the response was missing a required JSON key, add an assertion "
            "for that key in tests/integration/. "
            "The test must reproduce the failure against the pre-fix commit."
        ),
    },
    "latency_regression": {
        "add_to": "SLO monitoring (gate 5) + integration tests (gate 3)",
        "action": (
            "Lower the p99 latency SLO threshold or add a latency assertion in integration tests. "
            "If the regression was caused by a specific code path, add a targeted integration test "
            "for that path with a timeout assertion."
        ),
    },
    "cost_spike": {
        "add_to": "cost audit (gate 3)",
        "action": (
            "Lower the monthly cost threshold in scripts/cost_audit.py. "
            "Add a per-request token budget check in the integration fixture "
            "so that a single unusually expensive call is caught early."
        ),
    },
    "deployment_bug": {
        "add_to": "E2E tests (gate 4) + smoke tests",
        "action": (
            "Add an E2E test that reproduces the deployment-specific failure. "
            "Deployment bugs (missing env vars, CORS, routing) are typically caught "
            "only by gate 4; add a smoke test HTTP call that targets the specific endpoint."
        ),
    },
}

def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--type", choices=list(GATE_ADDITIONS.keys()), required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--description", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--affected_gate", type=int, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--root_cause", required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    addition = GATE_ADDITIONS[args.type]
    print(f"\n=== Post-Incident Gate Addition Checklist ===")
    print(f"Incident:    {args.description}")
    print(f"Root cause:  {args.root_cause}")
    print(f"Caught at:   Gate {args.affected_gate}")
    print(f"Add to:      {addition['add_to']}")
    print(f"\nAction required:")
    print(textwrap.fill(addition["action"], width=72, initial_indent="  ", subsequent_indent="  "))
    print()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Running this script after each incident post-mortem produces a concrete checklist rather than a vague action item to 'improve testing'. The output goes directly into the incident ticket as a set of linked sub-tasks, one per gate addition required.
Summary

Aligning tests to release-train stops makes each gate as cheap and fast as possible while catching the most likely regressions at the earliest point. Gate 1 (local pre-push) runs unit tests and lint in under 2 minutes. Gate 2 (PR CI) adds evals on prompt-affecting diffs, guarded by a budget check that skips the run when the monthly eval budget is exhausted. Gate 3 (post-merge) runs the full integration suite and a cost audit before the deploy pipeline starts. Gate 4 (staging) runs E2E tests and smoke calls after the deploy. Gate 5 (production) is continuous SLO monitoring that can trigger a kill switch automatically. Every production incident that was not caught by an existing gate requires a gate addition: a new eval case, a new integration assertion, or a lower SLO threshold. Non-blocking failures — latency in warning bands, flaky E2E tests, near-threshold cost — should alert but not halt the train; only confirmed regressions that breach defined thresholds block.
